coredata.dc:title: Chitosan nanocomposite drug delivery systems designed for the ifosfamide anticancer drug using molecular dynamics simulations
coredata.dc:creator: Shariatinia, Zahra
Mazloom-Jalali, Azin
coredata.dc:description: The capacity of chitosan (CS) nanocomposite systems containing graphene (G), N-doped graphene (GN) or P-doped graphene (GP) nanoparticles for the delivery of ifosfamide (IF) anticancer drug was examined by means of molecular dynamics (MD) simulations in order to find the most suitable drug delivery system (DDS). Moreover, the effect of temperature on the characteristics of these systems was investigated by running the MD simulations at four temperatures (298.15, 308.15, 318.15 and 328.15 K). The free volume (FV) and fractional free volume (FFV) values were increased by raising the temperature and the greatest FV and FFV were measured for the CS-GN-IF at 328.15 K equal to 11,751.73 Å3 and 40.9112%, respectively. The FV and FFV were changed in the order of CS-GN-IF > CS-GP-IF > CS-G-IF. The inter-chain distances at 328.15 K for the CS-G-IF, CS-GN-IF and CS-GP-IF were 0.9873, 0.8292 and 0.8856 Å, respectively, confirming the greatest intermolecular interactions in the CS-GN-IF due to formation of strong as well as weak hydrogen bonds among the polymer chains, GN NPs as well as IF molecules. The mean square displacement (MSD) and diffusion coefficient were enhanced by increasing the temperature. The greatest diffusion coefficient at 328.15 K (0.0693 × 10−5 cm2/s) was achieved for the CS-G-IF, a medium value was measured for the CS-GP-IF (0.0613 × 10−5 cm2/s) and the CS-GN-IF revealed the lowest diffusion coefficient (0.0565 × 10−5 cm2/s) approving the diffusion of drug in the CS-GN-IF was happened in the most controlled manner leading to the most efficient drug delivery.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molecular dynamics simulation
Drug delivery system
Chitosan nanocomposite
Anticancer drug
Fractional free volume
Mean square displacement

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Equilibrium and relaxation of amorphous cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The chemical structures of IF, CS, G, GN and GP species.

Snapshots of (a–d) CS-G-IF-25, CS-G-IF-35, CS-G-IF-45, CS-G-IF-55, (e–h) CS-GN-IF-25, CS-GN-IF-35, CS-GN-IF-45, CS-GN-IF-55 and (i–k) CS-GP-IF-25, CS-GP-IF-35, CS-GP-IF-45, CS-GP-IF-55 polymeric drug delivery systems obtained after production MD simulation runs.
The chemical structures and volumes (indicated by blue color) of the (a) G, (b) GN and (c) GP species.

The variations of kinetic, potential and non-bond energies as well as temperature by simulation time for the duration of 1 ns production step in the MD simulation runs for diverse polymeric membranes.

The free volumes (indicated by blue color) for the (a-d) CS-G-IF-25, CS-G-IF-35, CS-G-IF-45, CS-G-IF-55, (e-h) CS-GN-IF-25, CS-GN-IF-35, CS-GN-IF-45, CS-GN-IF-55 and (i-k) CS-GP-IF-25, CS-GP-IF-35, CS-GP-IF-45, CS-GP-IF-55 polymeric drug delivery systems.
The radius of gyration curves for the (a) CS-G-IF, (b) CS-GN-IF and (c) CS-GP-IF systems.
The XRD patterns for the (a) CS-G-IF, (b) CS-GN-IF and (c) CS-GP-IF systems in the 2θ range of 0–120°.
The RDFs for the intermolecular interactions occurring in the CS-G-IF drug delivery system.
The RDFs for the intermolecular interactions occurring in the CS-GN-IF drug delivery system.
The RDFs for the intermolecular interactions occurring in the CS-GP-IF drug delivery system.
The MSD diagrams for the diffusion of IF drug molecules inside the CS-G-IF, CS-GN-IF and CS-GP-IF nanocomposite drug delivery systems.
A summary of molecular dynamics simulations data acquired at 1 atm and different temperatures for the chitosan nanocomposite systems.
The RDFs (Ǻ) for the intermolecular interactions occurred between polymeric chains, IF drug and graphene at different temperatures inside the chitosan-graphene-ifosfamide (CS-G-IF) nanocomposite DDS.

The RDFs (Ǻ) for the intermolecular interactions occurred between polymeric chains, IF drug and graphene at different temperatures inside the chitosan-N-doped graphene-ifosfamide (CS-GN-IF) nanocomposite DDS.

The RDFs (Ǻ) for the intermolecular interactions occurred between polymeric chains, IF drug and graphene at different temperatures inside the chitosan-P-doped graphene-ifosfamide (CS-GP-IF) nanocomposite DDS.
Solubility parameters (J·cm−3)0.5 measured at different temperatures for the polymeric nanocomposite systems and their constituents.
Diffusion coefficients (×10−5 cm2/s) measured at different temperatures for the IF drug molecules inside the polymeric nanocomposite DDSs.
A comparison of the membranes properties examined in this work with those reported in literature.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: GO = graphene oxide. For GO-16 and GO-13, the final oxygen to carbon ratios obtained for the GO sheets were 1:6 and 1:3, respectively.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: e
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: It is an anionic copolymer based on methacrylic acid and methyl methacrylate at a ratio of 1:2 with pKa value of 6.66 ± 0.05 which is provided by Röhm pharma polymers.

head.ce:title: Chitosan nanocomposite drug delivery systems designed for the ifosfamide anticancer drug using molecular dynamics simulations

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Amirkabir University of Technology (Tehran Polytechnic), P.O.Box:15875-4413, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The capacity of chitosan (CS) nanocomposite systems containing graphene (G), N-doped graphene (GN) or P-doped graphene (GP) nanoparticles for the delivery of ifosfamide (IF) anticancer drug was examined by means of molecular dynamics (MD) simulations in order to find the most suitable drug delivery system (DDS). Moreover, the effect of temperature on the characteristics of these systems was investigated by running the MD simulations at four temperatures (298.15, 308.15, 318.15 and 328.15 K). The free volume (FV) and fractional free volume (FFV) values were increased by raising the temperature and the greatest FV and FFV were measured for the CS-GN-IF at 328.15 K equal to 11,751.73 Å3 and 40.9112%, respectively. The FV and FFV were changed in the order of CS-GN-IF > CS-GP-IF > CS-G-IF. The inter-chain distances at 328.15 K for the CS-G-IF, CS-GN-IF and CS-GP-IF were 0.9873, 0.8292 and 0.8856 Å, respectively, confirming the greatest intermolecular interactions in the CS-GN-IF due to formation of strong as well as weak hydrogen bonds among the polymer chains, GN NPs as well as IF molecules. The mean square displacement (MSD) and diffusion coefficient were enhanced by increasing the temperature. The greatest diffusion coefficient at 328.15 K (0.0693 × 10−5 cm2/s) was achieved for the CS-G-IF, a medium value was measured for the CS-GP-IF (0.0613 × 10−5 cm2/s) and the CS-GN-IF revealed the lowest diffusion coefficient (0.0565 × 10−5 cm2/s) approving the diffusion of drug in the CS-GN-IF was happened in the most controlled manner leading to the most efficient drug delivery.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is known as one of the leading causes of human mortality which results in almost 13% of human death [1]. Even though numerous efforts are being made to advance the cancer therapies, number of new cancers is still increasing [2]. Unfortunately, the classical cancer therapies such as chemotherapy were ineffective due to their side effects, poor cellular uptake and drug resistance. Therefore, it is necessary to design and develop new drug delivery systems (DDSs) to decrease these drawbacks and enhance efficiencies [3–7]. A DDS is a formulation, dosage form or device that can introduce a drug into the body to improve its efficacy and safety through controlling the rate and place of drug release [8–12].

Anticancer drugs are often loaded to nanoparticles to attain a specific drug delivery and to avoid the easily elimination [13,14]. Graphene based nanomaterials have emerged as favorable drug carriers for targeted DDSs [14]. They have attracted great attention due to their biocompatibility, good endocytosis and large surface area for loading drugs [1]. Functionalized graphene materials are suitably dispersed in water and physiological media [15]. Moreover, because the graphene-based particles have delocalized electrons, unparalleled excitation relevant emission, dispersion in various solvents, chemical inertness, capability of drug loading by π-π interactions and variable sizes and shapes, they have broadly been examined in drug delivery [16]. Furthermore, their structures are mainly composed of carbon element that has low cytotoxicity compared to inorganic metal/metal chalcogenide particles [17,18].

In order to increase the biocompatibility of graphene and its derivatives in biomedical applications, its composites with different polymers are usually prepared. Among diverse biopolymers, chitosan (CS) has drawn attention in pharmaceutical and biomedical applications due to its bioavailability, mucoadhesivity, biocompatibility, biodegradability, non-toxicity and low-immunogenicity [19–22]. Chitosan is a linear polysaccharide composed of β-(1–4)-linked d-glucosamine and N-acetyl-d-glucosamine units obtained by deacetylation of chitin [23]. It is a natural cationic polysaccharide presenting in the exoskeleton of crustaceans such as shrimps and crabs [24]. It is one of the most abundant biopolymers in nature after cellulose. Although CS is insoluble in most organic solvents, it is simply soluble in dilute acidic solutions since it has primary amino groups (pKa = 6.3) [25]. The existence of the amino groups point to the pH substantially changes the charged state and properties of CS [26]. At low pH, the amines are protonated and become positively charged, making CS a water-soluble cationic polyelectrolyte [27]. CS is degraded in vertebrates by lysozyme and bacterial enzymes in the colon. These properties make CS as an exceptional carrier for anticancer drugs.

Ifosfamide (IF, Fig. 1 ) is a chemotherapy medication used to treat numerous types of cancer including soft tissue sarcoma, testicular cancer, osteosarcoma, small cell lung cancer, bladder cancer, cervical cancer, ovarian cancer, endometrial, breast and testicular cancer as well as Hodgkin's disease [28]. Ifosfamide, like cyclophosphamide and trofosfamide, belongs to the phosphoramides family [29–37], which are powerfully alkylating compounds applied in cancer chemotherapy. IF is intrinsically inactive and requires to be activated in vivo to 4-hydroxyifosfamide which is in equilibrium with aldofosfamide. These compounds can be transformed to inactive or cytotoxic products through several metabolic pathways [38]. At present, IF has received increasing attention in the pharmaceutical and biomedical fields.

Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations are broadly accomplished to understand physical phenomena occurring in complex membranes at the molecular level [39–42]. The MD simulations explore the intermolecular interactions of polymeric carriers and drug molecules in polymeric nanocomposite drug delivery platforms [43,44]. Up to now, little computational simulations have been reported on the delivery of anticancer drugs using polymeric nanocomposites as drug delivery vehicles [45–47]. Therefore, we were interested to perform MD simulations on the delivery of anticancer ifosfamide (IF) molecules using chitosan (CS) polymeric composites filled by three graphene (G), N-doped graphene (GN) and P-doped graphene (GP) nanoparticles. Thus, the diffusion behavior of IF into the CS-G-IF, CS-GN-IF and CS-GP-IF matrixes was studied using the MD simulations. Besides, the influence of temperature (at 298.15, 308.15, 318.15 and 328.15 K) on the interaction energies between diffused molecules and polymeric chains, FV, FFV, radius of gyration, X-ray diffraction, solubility parameter and diffusion coefficients were evaluated and compared with each other in order to determine which nanocomposite system would be the most suitable one to deliver IF molecules.

For both of the minimization and dynamics steps, the COMPASS force field (FF), which is condensed-phase optimized molecular potentials, commonly used in atomistic simulations, was employed for all of molecules. The COMPASS FF is based on the Polymer Consistent Force Field (PCFF) [48,49] which is the first FF validated and parameterized by means of condensed-phase criteria, ab initio and empirical data existing for numerous molecules. Beside the non-bonded and bonded interactions which are employed in other FFs, the COMPASS also considers cross coupling contacts, consequently enabling exact estimation of various properties for a number of materials mainly for polymers [50,51].

Charge equilibration approach was selected for the calculation of partial atomic charges on molecules [52]. Summation of valence (bond), non-bond and cross-term interactions gave total energy for a system which is written as:
E total
=
E valence
+
E
non − bond
+
E
cross − term

The Evalence includes bond stretching (Ebond), bending of valence angle (Eangle), torsion of dihedral angle (Etorsion) plus inversion (or out of plane interaction) represents as Einversion or Eoop. Furthermore, modern FFs such as COMPASS comprise Urey-Bradley term (EUB) which denotes interactions between atomic pairs within 1–3 configurations (for atoms binding to an atom):
E valence
=
E torsion
+
E angle
+
E bond
+
E UB
+
E oop

Cross terms occurring in Ecrossterm enhance the FF accuracy employing the correction factors onto valence energy in order to show the interdependence between diverse valence terms. As an example, the term Ebond-bond considers the stretching-stretching interactions between two adjacent bonds. Likewise, the COMPASS FF is comprised of bending-bending, stretching-bending, bending-torsion stretching-torsion and bending-bending-torsion terms. The term Enon-bond specifies interactions between non-bonded nuclei (or secondary interactions) and it is composed of three Coulomb electrostatic (ECoulomb), van der Waals (EvdW) as well as hydrogen bond (EH-bond) energies:
E
non − bond
=
E Coulomb
+
E vdW
+
E
H − bond

The EvdW in COMPASS is defined by means of Lennard-Jones 6–12 potential. The charge-equilibration route is utilized to measure partial charges on atoms. The Columbic long-range contacts are obtained using the Ewald method [53].

Each particle was first built using the molecule modelling tool; then, minimizations were conducted in order to attain the structures with the minimum energies. For this purpose, every particle was initially minimized by the steepest descent method with the convergence of 1000 kcal/mol, next by the conjugate gradient method with the convergence of 10 kcal/mol and finally by the Newton method with convergence of 0.1 kcal/mol.

All amorphous cells were composed of three IF drug molecules, one graphene particle (G, GN or GP) as filler and five chitosan chains having twenty repeating units (as the polymeric matrix) to investigate the effects of these DDSs containing three kinds of fillers on the drug delivery efficiency. The chemical structures of IF, CS, G, GN and GP species are presented in Fig. 1. The 3–D cells were run using the MD simulations at standard temperature and pressure of 298.15 K and 1 atm, respectively. All initial amorphous cells were created at a very low density of 0.15 g cm−3 to construct the cells having periodic boundary conditions by means of amorphous cell module provided in Materials Studio software (version 4.3) [54]. Such an initial density was chosen to better equilibrate the organized cells. Table 1 presents complete compositions for the cells as well as a summary of the simulation results.

First, the energy minimizations of all configurations in amorphous cells were performed with the smart minimizer algorithm which combines conjugate gradient, steepest descent and Newton minimization algorithms. The minimization was carried out in a cascading manner for 200,000 iterations to reach relaxation for overlapping atoms and the convergence criteria were set at 1000 and 0.1 kcal/(mol·Å), respectively. Subsequent to the initial optimization step, next dynamics simulations were accomplished to completely relax the simulation system. Hence, the NVT simulation was firstly done at 298.15 K for 50 ps. Then, the NPT simulation was performed at 298.15 K and 1 atm for 50 ps. This approach aimed to wholly relax the 3-D cells and speed up fulfillment of their ultimate correct density. The effect of temperature on the drug delivery was estimated by running the MD simulations at four different temperatures (298.15, 308.15, 318.15 and 328.15 K). During the relaxation step, non-bond interactions (i.e., electrostatic potentials and van der Waals) were measured by Ewald summation method. Afterward, accomplishment of MD simulation with NVT ensemble at 298.15 K was done at the relaxed density for 3 ns employing cell multipole method (CMM). As a last point, the final 1 ns trajectories were applied for succeeding analysis of dynamical, structural and energetic properties [55]. The velocity Verlet algorithm was used to unravel the classical motion equation with a 1 fs time step [56]. The application of Berendsen barostat and thermostat procedure with a 0.1 ps decay constant for all NPT and NVT simulations was due to maintaining the pressure and temperature at the selected values in the period of the MD runs [57].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Equilibrium and relaxation of amorphous cells
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the properties of chitosan polymeric DDSs containing graphene nanoparticles (G, GN or GP), their relaxations and equilibrations were evaluated and compared with each other. For this purpose, some relaxation properties such as temperature plus kinetic, potential and non-bond energies were estimated in the period of the MD simulations. The relaxed configurations of the polymer composites achieved after 1 ns production runs at different temperatures are shown in Fig. 2 . Table 1 presents the computed bulk densities and dimensions (cell sizes) for the amorphous simulation cells comprising polymer composites. Evidently, the cell size and thus the cell volume are increased from the CS-G-IF system to CS-GN-IF and CS-GP-IF so that the greatest cell size is measured for the CS-GP-IF nanocomposite. The changes in cell dimensions may be attributed to the greatest size of P atom in the GP species. The volumes of the G, GN and GP particles are measured equal to 250.99, 252.97 and 255.45 Ǻ3, respectively, which confirms the GP occupies the greatest volume among the three nanocomposite systems. The chemical structures and volumes (designated by blue color) of the G, GN and GP species are presented in Fig. 3 . It is observed that the volume of the graphene species varies in the order G < GN < GP.

The bulk density of the CS-G-IF matrix at 298.15 K is 1.2914 g/cm3 while it is increased to 1.3115 and 1.3012 g/cm3 in CS-GN-IF and CS-GP-IF systems, respectively. Therefore, it is found that the density of nanocomposite systems changes in the order of CS-GN-IF > CS-GP-IF > CS-G-IF indicating the highest density for the CS-GN-IF. The reason for this observation can be related to the changes in the occupied volumes of the nanocomposites so that the density has an opposite relationship with the occupied volume of the system. Similar results are observed for the density values of CS-G-IF, CS-GN-IF and CS-GP-IF nanocomposites measured at higher temperatures (see Table 1). Comparing the densities of CS-G-IF at various temperatures illustrates a linear relationship between the density and temperature so that the density is enhanced by raising the temperature. These results are in agreement with the values reported for the CS composites. For instance, the density values measured for the CS were 1.42 [58] and 1.40 g/cm3 [59]. Therefore, the simulated densities for these polymeric nanocomposites are reasonable. This can be accounted for as another indication of achieving cells equilibrations.

For all of entirely optimized systems, the variations in the kinetic, potential and non-bond energies as well as temperature versus simulation time are exhibited in Fig. 4 reflecting the changes are very much small and the systems have reached the relaxation states. A comparison of the kinetic, potential and non-bond energies of CS-G-IF, CS-GN-IF and CS-GP-IF systems reveals that CS-GN-IF composite provides the smallest energies, then CS-GP-IF has medium energies and finally the greatest values belong to the CS-G-IF system. Besides, the kinetic energies of CS-G-IF, CS-GN-IF and CS-GP-IF DDSs are the smallest among other energies that are approximately 2020, 1710 and 1870 kcal/mol, respectively. The potential energies of the three CS-G-IF, CS-GN-IF and CS-GP-IF systems are around 4810, 4500 and 4660 kcal/mol but their corresponding non-bond energies are about 5160, 4870 and 5020 kcal/mol, respectively. Consequently, it may be concluded that the CS-GN-IF with the least energies can be chosen as the most appropriate DDS. Nonetheless, in order to select the most apposite DDS with the most effective drug delivery, other features of the polymeric composites must be assessed and compared with each other. Consequently, in next sections, relationships between nanocomposite system type and temperature with the IF delivery efficacy will be analyzed to decide which DDS will be the most suitable candidate to be used in the drug delivery application.

The surface areas of the polymeric DDSs are given in Table 1. It is seen that the surface areas of CS-G-IF, CS-GN-IF and CS-GP-IF systems at 298.15 K are equal to 7853.84, 8335.72 and 8436.58 Å2, respectively confirming the surface area changes in the order CS-G-IF < CS-GN-IF < CS-GP-IF. This result can be associated with the greatest surface area of GP. A similar trend is seen when comparing the surface areas of these systems at higher temperatures. Also, it is found that the surface area is enhanced by increasing the temperature from 298.15 to 328.15 K in all of the nanocomposite systems. For example, the surface areas of the CS-G-IF at 308.15, 318.15 and 328.15 K are equal to 7989.72, 8168.93 and 8309.27 Ǻ2, respectively.

Two distinct phases which happen in a polymeric matrix include solid phase polymer chains in addition to other species plus an unfilled space which is recognized as the FV [60]. It is known that diffusion into a polymeric membrane matrix is dependent to its FV and morphology. The FV is measured as the summation of the static holes created by packing of chains or transient gaps generated through thermally rearranged chains so that a low-resistance transporting path is provided for the diffusing molecules [61]. Usually, the FV has a straight influence on diffusivity of polymers and increasing the FV facilitates the molecular diffusivity [62]. The Connolly surface approach was employed to determine the FV for the polymeric nanocomposites. In this method, probe fragment was modeled by a hard sphere having a radius equal to 0.1 nm. The FV attained using hard sphere probe markedly depends on the probe size.

Table 1 provides the FV values for the simulation cells containing CS-G-IF, CS-GN-IF and CS-GP-IF polymeric composites. As well, Fig. 5 displays the FVs by blue color for these DDSs. It is recognized that at 298.15 K, the FV is the greatest in the CS-GN-IF system (11,465.68 Å3), it is decreased to 11,296.71 Å3 in CS-GP-IF nanocomposite and the lowest value is obtained for the CS-G-IF composite (11,215.29 Å3). Similar results are observed at higher temperatures. Also, the FV is enhanced by raising the temperature from 298.15 to 328.15 K.

The fractional free volume (FFV) is the ratio of FV to the entire volume of the cell. It is observed that at 298.15 K, CS-GN-IF have the highest FFV (39.9154%), the CS-GP-IF gives a medium FFV value of 39.2819% and finally the CS-G-IF presents the least FFV (39.0868%). The FFV values vary similar to the FV values and these results approve the data acquired for the cell size and cell volume. For instance, the FFV of CS-GN-IF system at 308.15, 318.15 and 328.15 K are 40.0437, 40.4263 and 40.9112% displaying higher temperature causes easier movement of polymeric chains which can lead to more distances between them. Thus, at higher temperature the voids/pores between the polymeric chains are increased which brings about enhanced FV and FFV values.

The highest FFV value of CS-GN-IF nanocomposite system may indicate that the transport and diffusion of the IF drug inside this system should take place the most easily and it may be anticipated that the highest diffusion coefficient can be measured for this DDS. The reason for the decreased FV and FFV values in the CS-G-IF and CS-GP-IF systems can be associated to the restrained segmental mobility of polymeric chains which are close to the graphene–polymer interface which produces several nanosized holes that are proper places for molecular diffusion/transport.

A rise in the FV and FFV generally results in a more effectual penetration in the polymeric membranes [63]. For example, the correlation between FV properties (size and distribution) in the interior of polyamide (PA) active layer and mass transport mechanism, six polyamide thin film composite membranes were characterized using positron annihilation lifetime spectroscopy (PALS) [64]. It was revealed that under pressurized filtration condition, the rejection rate of boron across all PA membranes was inversely proportional to the FV size and more boron transport occurred at the membrane containing larger free volume [64]. In another work, blend membranes of polynorbornene and 2,2′-bis(3,4′-dicarboxyphenyl)hexafluoropropane dianhydride-durene were fabricated to improve H2 permselectivity and it was found that the structural changes by blending increased permeability due to increasing the FFV of polymer blends [65]. Also, the separation efficiencies of polyamide membranes were significantly related with their FV properties [66].

The FV and the FFV of the CS-GN-IF are the maximum values among all composites but those of the CS-G-IF are the smallest values, thus it may be predicted that the highest diffusion rates will be distinguished for IF molecules within the CS-GN-IF system. Nevertheless, in order to determine the most appropriate nanocomposite DDS, only the FV and FFV values cannot be relied on and more parameters including radius of gyration, X-ray diffraction pattern, radial distribution function and mean square displacement must be studied and compared with each other.

The radius of gyration is used to describe the dimension of a polymer chain which is calculated by the following equation [67] where mi and ri are the mass and position vector of atom i with respect to the mass center of the molecule. It is known that the Rg 2 is defined as the mean square of the distance between the beads and the center of mass that means a greater Rg reveals a greater distance between the polymeric chain and its center of mass [67].
R g
=
∑ i
r i
2
mi
∑ i
mi

The gyration radii for all polymeric DDSs have been given in Table 1 and Fig. 6 . It is seen that at 298.15 K, the CS-GN-IF has the least Rg value (2.99 Å), then CS-GP-IF exhibits the Rg value of 3.08 and the CS-G-IF provides the greatest Rg = 3.11 Å. Similar results are achieved at higher temperatures. These data indicate that the polymeric chains in the CS-GN-IF have the closest contacts and intermolecular interactions with each other which lead to their highest proximity. This can result in the greatest FV and FFV values for the CS-GN-IF nanocomposite system among the three DDSs. Furthermore, the Rg value is enhanced by raising the temperature. For example, the Rg values for the CS-GN-IF at 308.15, 318.15 and 328.15 K are 3.03, 3.07 and 3.12 Å, respectively. This result supports that higher temperature can result in easier movement of polymeric chains, thus it can lead to an enhanced Rg value. Accordingly, it may be decided that IF drug may be the most effectively delivered using the CS-GN-IF system at higher temperature having the highest FV and leads to the most facilitated passage of the drug molecules within this system. A comparison with the experimental data shows that the Rg of CS when chains in the unperturbed state was equal to 5.25 Å [68]. Another group reported that the Rg for the CS was equal to 2.7 Å [69]. It is notable that the Rg is influenced by the degree of polymerization (DP) so that the Rg was varied in the range of 3.44 to 8.25 Å for the CS polymer with DP changing from 646 to 2100 [70].

The simulated scattering intensity curve, I(Q), for each membrane is related to the radial distribution function through a Fourier transform operation and is measured from the equation below [40,41] where θ is the scattering angle, λ is the X-ray wavelength and the magnitude of the scattering angle Q =
4 π sin θ
λ
. The indices j and k range over all the atoms in the molecule.
I
Q
=
∑ j
∑ k
f j
f k
sin
Qr jk
Qr jk

By applying the scattering analysis from the Forcite module of the simulation software, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of all membranes were obtained. The angle of diffraction was varied from 0° to 120° using a step size of 0.05°. In order to calculate d-spacing values that are representative of inter-segmental distances between polymer backbones, the maximum peak of the diffraction pattern is commonly used.

Fig. 7 displays the XRD patterns for the polymeric nanocomposite systems. The XRD diagrams could be accounted for the crystallinity of a compound by comparing the background integrated intensity to the intensities of sharp signals. A crystalline material contains several sharp peaks but an amorphous material (such as liquids and glasses) shows a broad background signal. It is clear from the XRD patterns in Fig. 7 that there is one sharp peak at about 10° along with a broad peak near 20° confirming the presence of CS, G/GN/GP and IF in all of the nanocomposite systems. Similar result was reported in literature in which the graphene displayed its characteristic XRD peak at 2θ ≈ 23° [71]. The CS indicates its corresponding XRD peaks at 2θ values of about 10 and 20°. This pattern corresponds to the reported patterns in literature confirming purity of the sample [72]. It is notable that the crystallinity of CS is due to the formation of hydrogen bonds between its amino, hydroxyl and N-acetyl groups leading to the appearance of sharp peaks in the X-ray crystallographic pattern which are generated from parallel and anti-parallel alignments of polymeric chains or sheets. The XRD pattern of semi-crystalline CS exhibited both amorphous and crystalline regions [73]. Also, it was reported that at room temperature (25 °C), the XRD pattern of non-neutralized CS film showed a broad peak centered at ~18° plus two sharp peaks near 9° and 11° that were indicative of its semi-crystalline nature [73].

It is observed that the intensity of the XRD peaks are the lowest for the CS-GN-IF but it increases in case of CS-G-IF and CS-GP-IF with almost comparable intensity is measured for the CS-G-IF and CS-GP-IF systems. This may be related to the lowest crystallinity of CS-GN-IF while increased crystallinity of other systems. Further, at 298.15 K, the position of the XRD peak at 2θ ~ 10° moves to smaller 2θ value from CS-GN-IF nanocomposite (12.45°) to the CS-GP-IF (11.62°) and CS-G-IF (10.27°) which can be described by the smallest inter-chain distance in CS-GN-IF but the greatest one in CS-G-IF. Analogous results are acquired at higher temperatures. Also, increasing the temperature leads to deceasing the 2θ value. For example, the diffraction angles for the CS-GN-IF nanocomposite at 298.15, 308.15, 318.15 and 328.15 K are equal to 12.45, 11.79, 11.14 and 10.66°, respectively.

The inter-chain distances were calculated by means of the Bragg equation (λ = 2dsinθ, λ = 1.5406 Å). The maximum peaks in the XRD diagrams and the d-spacing values for the composite DDSs are given in Table 1. It is seen that at 298.15 K, the inter-chain distances for the CS-G-IF, CS-GN-IF and CS-GP-IF are 0.8606, 0.7104 and 0.7609 Å, respectively. Comparable results are obtained at higher temperatures. The drop in the d-spacing may be accounted for greater intermolecular interactions due to formation of the strong as well as weak hydrogen bonds among the polymer chains, G/GN/GP nanoparticle as well as IF molecules. This data agrees well with the FV and FFV results so that the CS-GN-IF illustrates the largest FV and FFV among all of the DDSs, which is due to the greatest inter-particle interactions resulting in the highest FV and FFV values.

In order to evaluate the interaction distances within the polymeric DDSs, the RDF also known as pair correlation function, gA–B(r) was analyzed. RDF indicates the possibility of distributing B atoms about A atoms as reference. The gA–B(r) can mathematically be written by the subsequent equation, where nB is the number of B atoms positioned around A atoms in a spherical shell with Δr thickness, NB is the whole number of B atoms engaged in the amorphous cell and V is the cell volume.
g
A − B
r
=
n B
4 π
r 2
Δr
N B
V

Comparing different RDFs could be accounted for weak, moderate and strong interactions between the particles. Figs. 8–10
depict various RDF plots at four different temperatures for the inter-atomic interactions occurred inside the CS-G-IF, CS-GN-IF and CS-GP-IF drug delivery systems. It is notable that the N(endo-IF)–H(OH-CS) means the distance between endocyclic N atom of IF and H atom of the hydroxyl group of the CS. This kind of definition is also applicable for other types of RDF distances presented in Figs. 8–10. Besides, the RDF values for the intermolecular interactions happened between CS polymeric chains, IF drug and G/GN/GP inside the polymeric nanocomposite DDSs are provided in Tables 2–4
.

It can be recognized that at four temperatures in all CS-G-IF, CS-GN-IF and CS-GP-IF DDSs, the strongest interactions are drug-polymer, then graphene-polymer and finally the weakest interactions are graphene-drug. In other words, such interactions vary in the order drug-polymer > graphene-polymer > graphene-drug. This can be attributed to the strongest hydrogen bond and electrostatic interactions occurred between the functional groups of drug and polymer. Since the pristine graphene does not have any functional groups, it does not have the ability to very strongly interact with drug molecules and polymeric chains. Thus, the graphene-polymer and graphene-drug interactions are both weaker than those of the drug-polymer. Comparing the graphene-polymer and graphene-drug indicates that the graphene-polymer interactions are very stronger which can be related to the greater number of functional groups existing on the polymeric chains compared with those of drug molecules leading to more efficient graphene-polymer interactions. As an example, in the CS-G-IF system at 298.15 K, the O(P = O)-H(OH-CS) distance is 2.30 Å which can be accounted for the drug-polymer interaction and the H(OH-CS)-C(G) and O(P = O)-C(G) distances are 2.28 and 3.65 Å which are considered as the graphene-polymer and graphene-drug interactions, respectively. At 298.15 K, the O(P = O)-H(OH-CS), H(OH-CS)-C(GN) and O(P = O)-C(GN) distances are equal to 2.22, 2.28 and 9.19 Å for the CS-GN-IF, respectively. The RDFs of O(P = O)-H(OH-CS), H(OH-CS)-C(GP) interactions and O(P = O)-C(GP) at 298.15 K are 2.26, 2.30 and 7.81 Å for the CS-GP-IF system, respectively. These data confirm the above results that the strongest interactions take place between drug and polymer chains. Similar results are also achieved at higher temperatures. However, the RFD values are increased by raising the temperature which is due to the easier movements of all species including IF molecules, G/Gn/GP nanoparticles and CS polymeric chains at higher temperatures that can result in greater distances (RDFs) between the interacting atoms.

A comparison of the graphene-polymer interactions happened between the functional groups of polymer with carbon and nitrogen atoms of GN exhibits that the interactions with the nitrogen atom of GN are very stronger compared with those occurred with its carbon atoms. Likewise, the interactions with the phosphorus atom of GP are much stronger than those with its carbon atom. For instance, the RDF values at 298.15 K for the O(P = O)-C(GN), O(P = O)-N(GN) and O(P = O)-C(GP), O(P = O)-P(GP) interactions are 9.19, 2.59 Å and 7.81, 3.06 Å, respectively. Comparable results are acquired when considering the graphene-drug interactions. Besides, RDF data in Tables 3 and 4 exhibit that the interactions with the N atom of GN are stronger that those with P atom of GP which give smaller RDF values for the former interactions. This result can be related to the greater electronegativity of N compared with that of P atom which results in stronger H-bonding and electrostatic interaction with the nitrogen atom.

It was mentioned that the drug-polymer interactions are the strongest in all of the three DDSs and the smallest drug-polymer RDFs belong to the CS-GN-IF, then CS-GP-IF and finally to the CS-G-IF system. This result approves that the strongest hydrogen bonding interactions take place in the CS-GN-IF but the weakest ones happen in the CS-G-IF DDS. Consequently, it can be inferred from the RDF data that the IF molecules can the most readily diffuse in the CS-GN-IF, moderately cross the CS-GP-IF while the most weakly transport within the CS-G-IF system especially at higher temperatures. Hence, considering the RDF results, it may be anticipated that the CS-GN-IF would be selected as the most appropriate DDS.

The solubility parameter (δ) is a numerical estimation of the interaction degree between materials indicating their solubility, mainly for polymers [74]. It is used in industry to predict the permeation, compatibility, swelling, bulk/solution characteristics of polymers [74]. For a pure compound, it is defined as the square root of the cohesive energy density (ECED) from Hildebrand-Scatchard solution theory (Eq. (1)).
(1)
δ =
E CED

The E
CED is the energy required to totally remove unit volume of molecules from their neighbors to infinite separation (an ideal gas), which is equivalent to the molar vaporization energy (ΔHvap–RT) divided by molar volume (V) (Eq. (2)), where ΔH is the molar vaporization enthalpy, R is the gas constant and E is the vaporization energy; all physical parameters are measured at the same temperature (T).
(2)
δ =
∆ H
vap
− RT
V
=
E V

The Hildebrand parameter is divided to three Hansen parameters including dispersion, polar and hydrogen bonding forces which are distinct δd, δp and δH solubility parameters, respectively [75]. The δ for a polymer is a thermophysical property that is greatly dependent to the temperature.
(3)
δ =
δ d 2
+
δ h 2
+
δ p 2

Solubility parameters for the polymeric nanocomposite DDSs and their constituents measured at different temperatures are presented in Table 5 . It is observed that in all cases, the δ is decreased by increasing temperature. This can be explained by enhancement in the molecular volume at higher temperature which leads to decreasing the δ according to Eq. (2). It is noteworthy that although at higher T the dispersion energy is highly increased among other energies, the volume enhancement is dominant. Moreover, it is obvious from Table 5 that the solubility parameters of IF drug, G, GN and GP are almost close to that of CS reflecting these species are compatible with each other and they can suitably become miscible. Comparing the δ values of G, GN and GP reveals that GN has the greatest value but the GP provides the smallest one. For instance, the δ values at 298.15 K are 14.953, 15.268 and 14.306 (J·cm−3)0.5 for the G, GN and GP, respectively. The greatest δ for the GN can be accounted for the highest electronegativity of N among C, N and P elements leading to the highest polar (dipole) and hydrogen bonding interactions. The smallest value for the GP may be related to it's the greatest volume resulting in the lowest δ (see Eq. (2)).

It is found that the CS-G-IF and CS-GN-IF systems exhibit the smallest and greatest δ values and a medium value is achieved for the CS-GP-IF. As an example, the CS-G-IF, CS-GN-IF and CS-GP-IF indicate δ values equal to 20.247, 24.483 and 23.398 (J·cm−3)0.5, respectively, at 298.15 K. The least δ values are obtained for the CS-G-IF nanocomposite due to the pristine G particle does not have any functional groups to interact strongly with the CS and IF (as confirmed by the RDF data). The greatest solubility parameter for the CS-GN-IF verifies that strongest intermolecular interactions occur in this system which can result in the most controlled and slowest drug delivery. Hence, considering the solubility parameters, the priority to choose the most appropriate DDS changes as CS-GN-IF > CS-GP-IF > CS-G-IF.

The diffusivity values of IF molecules within the polymeric composite systems were computed by plotting their MSD curves. Also, the estimation of diffusion coefficient values for the IF molecules was made using the slopes of these curves. Fig. 11 presents the MSD diagrams for the diffusion of IF drug molecules inside the CS-G-IF, CS-GN-IF and CS-GP-IF nanocomposite drug delivery systems at four different temperatures. The nearly linear lines in the MSD plots infer constant diffusions of IF into the DDSs for the period of the MD simulations. It is found that the CS-G-IF provides the highest MSD for the IF diffusion, a medium diffusion is observed for the CS-GP-IF system and the CS-GN-IF affords the smallest MSD. Furthermore, the MSD values are increased by raising the temperature. Therefore, in order to proficiently deliver the IF drug, a DDS with the lowest MSD must be selected. This leads in the most controlled and slowest transport/diffusion of IF molecules inside the CS-GN-IF system while the fastest passage in the CS-G-IF nanocomposite. As it is evident from Fig. 11, the two plots reflect that the CS-GN-IF is the most efficient and appropriate nanocomposite system for the drug delivery application. Table 6 lists the diffusion coefficients for the IF molecules into the three polymeric nanocomposite systems at four different temperatures. These data confirm the MSD curves so that the highest diffusion coefficient is achieved at 328.15 K for the CS-G-IF (0.0693 × 10−5 cm2/s), a medium value is achieved for the CS-GP-IF (0.0613 × 10−4 cm2/s) and the lowest IF diffusion coefficient is measured for the CS-GN-IF (0.0565 × 10−4 cm2/s). Accordingly, since the diffusion of IF drug in the CS-GN-IF is the slowest, it leads to the most efficient drug delivery. This result approves that the ability of the three examined systems for the delivery of the IF drug changes in the order of CS-GN-IF > CS-GP-IF > CS-G-IF. The reason for such phenomenon can be attributed to the presence of N and P doped graphene in the CS-GN-IF and CS-GP-IF systems but in the CS-G-IF, the pristine graphene has been used. The existence of N or P atoms in the functionalized graphene can lead to more efficient interactions (H-bonding and electrostatic) of GN and GP with the IF drug and CS polymeric chains. Thus, the drug diffusion can occur more effectively within these systems compared with the CS-G-IF containing pure G nanoparticle as the filler.

In order to compare our data with those reported in literature, the diffusion coefficients of various penetrants (including drug and other types of molecules) within the polymeric membranes have been presented in Table 7 . It is observed that the diffusion coefficients measured for diverse penetrants in various membranes are of different orders of magnitudes which can be attributed to the penetrant size/shape, functional groups existing on both membrane species that could result in weak/strong intra- and intermolecular interactions.

The non-polymeric pristine graphene (PG) and graphene oxide (GO) were employed for the delivery of the well-known anticancer doxorubicin drug under three acidic (pH = 1–5), neutral (pH = 7) and alkaline (pH = 9–14) and the highest diffusion coefficient was achieved in alkaline medium and for the GO-16 compared with GO-13 which were different in their O/C ratios (16 and 13) [76]. For example, the diffusion coefficient values for the PG, GO-16 and GO-13 at pH = 9–14 were 0.166 × 10−4, 0.210 × 10−4 and 0.197 × 10−4 cm2/g, respectively [76]. Such diffusion coefficients are almost two orders of magnitude larger than those of our CS-G-IF, CS-GN-IF and CS-GP-IF systems. The PVA-10%PAA-x%GA (x = 2.5, 5, 7.5) membranes (PAA = poly(acrylic acid), GA = glutaraldehyde) presented diffusion coefficients of 0.395 × 10−4, 0.194 × 10−4 and 0.139 × 10−4 for the diclofenac sodium delivery at pH = 7.4 but smaller diffusion coefficients were attained at pH = 1.2 [77]. It is obvious that these diffusion coefficient values are two orders of magnitude greater than those of our DDSs.

The water swollen sodium alginate (SA) membranes produced using solutions comprising 1, 2 and 4% SA exhibited diffusion coefficients of 0.056 × 10−4, 0.043 × 10−4 and 0.050 × 10−4 cm2/s for the theophylline delivery, respectively [78]. A commercial DDS named Eudragit® S-100 which is an anionic copolymer based on MAA and MMA in 1:2 ratio with pKa = 6.66 ± 0.05 displayed a diffusion coefficient value of 0.057 × 10−4 cm2/g for 5-amino-2-hydroxybenzioc acid [79]. The poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) membranes were prepares to deliver theophylline and aspirin with molecular weights of 180.17 and 180.16 g/mol, respectively [80]. It was found that though the two drugs have almost the same molecular weights, they indicated dissimilar diffusion coefficients of 0.0127 × 10−6 and 0.029 × 10−8 cm2/s which were related to their properties such as their van der Waals volumes of 141.76 and 149.33 Ǻ3, respectively [80]. Poly(HEMA-co-MAA) and poly(HEMA-co-MMA) in which HEMA = 2-hydroxyethyl methacrylate, MAA = methacrylic acid and MMA = methyl methacrylate, were used for timolol maleate delivery and comparable diffusion coefficients (0.0149 × 10−6 and 0.0111 × 10−6) were acquired [81].

Last but not least, it should be stated that the diffusion coefficients of various penetrants in membranes compared here are different with some orders of magnitudes which can be related to the properties of both membrane and penetrant species.

The MD simulations were performed at four different temperatures on the CS-G-IF, CS-GN-IF and CS-GP-IF polymeric nanocomposite systems containing G, GN or GP nanoparticles in order to find the best DDS with the most proficient delivery of IF drug. The density was enhanced by raising the temperature from 298.15 to 328.15 K. The potential energies of the CS-GN-IF composite were the smallest values but the greatest energies belonged to the CS-G-IF system. It was established that at four temperatures, CS-GN-IF revealed the greatest FFV values, the CS-GP-IF had medium values and the CS-G-IF displayed the smallest FFVs which reflected the transport and diffusion of the IF drug inside the CS-GN-IF nanocomposite may occur the most easily. At four temperatures, the CS-GN-IF system had the least Rg values, the CS-GP-IF showed medium values and the CS-G-IF illustrated the greatest values. These data confirmed that the polymeric chains in the CS-GN-IF had the closest contacts and intermolecular interactions with each other which led to their highest proximity that could cause the greatest FV and FFV values for the CS-GN-IF nanocomposite among the three DDSs. The XRD patterns of all DDSs revealed a sharp peak at about 10° along with a broad peak near 20° that confirmed the presence of CS, G/GN/GP and IF in the nanocomposites. The RDF data proved that in all DDSs, the drug-polymer interactions were the strongest, the graphene-polymer were medium ones and the weakest interactions were graphene-drug so that they changed in the order drug-polymer > graphene-polymer > graphene-drug. It was also concluded from the RDF values that the IF molecules had the strongest interactions with the CS-GN-IF system, moderate interactions in the CS-GP-IF while the weakest interactions within the CS-G-IF nanocomposite which verified the drug molecules would diffuse the most easily inside the CS-G-IF system. The highest diffusion coefficient was attained for the CS-G-IF, a medium value was acquired for the CS-GP-IF and the lowest diffusion coefficient was obtained for the CS-GN-IF. This result verified that the diffusion of IF in the CS-GN-IF was the most controlled which could cause the most effective drug delivery.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors would like to appreciatively express their thanks to the research office of Amirkabir University of Technology (Tehran Polytechnic), Tehran, Iran for the financial support of this work. Also, we are grateful to High Performance Computing Cluster of Amirkabir University of Technology (Tehran Polytechnic), Tehran, Iran for affording the computational facilities (software and hardware) to accomplish the MD simulations.
